claim: Keep tests in a tests/ directory outside the application package — they run against the installed version after 'pip install .' and against the local copy with an editable install, and stay out of your distribution.

Put the application package under src/ (src/mypkg, not mypkg at repo root) — it prevents accidentally testing the repo checkout when you meant the installed package, the most common packaging pitfall.
Develop in a venv with 'pip install -e .' so code changes apply without reinstalling, with a pyproject.toml at the repo root declaring the package.

pytest collects test_*.py or *_test.py files, test_-prefixed functions, and test_-prefixed methods on Test-prefixed classes that have NO __init__ — a test class with __init__ is silently never collected.

key: General|Testing|Discovery|Unique test file names under prepend
claim: Under the default prepend import mode, test files import as top-level modules, so their names must be unique across the whole suite; use --import-mode=importlib for new projects, which leaves sys.path untouched and lifts that constraint.
Adding __init__.py files to tests/ permits same-named test modules but makes pytest prepend the repo root to sys.path — the local package shadows the installed one, defeating tools like tox.
Use tox (or equivalent) to run the suite against the INSTALLED package in a clean virtualenv before release — it catches packaging glitches that running from the checkout cannot.
Do not run tests through 'python setup.py test' or pytest-runner — both are deprecated and depend on setuptools features that bypass pip's security mechanisms like --require-hashes.

Enable pytest's strictness options (strict_markers, strict_config, or strict=true on pytest>=9) so marker typos and config errors fail loudly — and only blanket-enable strict with a pinned pytest version, since new options auto-activate.
Run a pytest-aware linter (flake8-pytest-style or the ruff PT rules) over the suite — fixture misuse, unregistered markers, and naming mistakes are bugs that ordinary linters don't see.
'python -m pytest' additionally puts the current directory on sys.path while bare 'pytest' does not — know which one your project's imports depend on and standardize it.
Write plain assert statements — pytest's assertion rewriting reports the values of subexpressions on failure, so 'assert result == expected' beats self.assertEqual and needs no message in most cases.
source: pytest docs: How to write and report assertions

claim: Assert expected exceptions with 'with pytest.raises(ValueError, match=...)' — asserting both the type and a message fragment pins the behavior; a bare try/except in a test silently passes when nothing raises.
source: pytest docs: How to write and report assertions

claim: Prefer fixtures to xunit-style setUp/tearDown — fixtures compose, declare their dependencies by name, scope explicitly (function/class/module/session), and yield-style fixtures guarantee cleanup after the yield.

Give expensive fixtures (database, server, model load) a wider scope (module/session) deliberately and keep mutable ones function-scoped — a session-scoped mutable fixture is shared state between tests.
Express case tables with @pytest.mark.parametrize instead of copy-pasted test functions — each case reports and fails independently, and adding a case is one line.
Write test files through the tmp_path fixture, never into the working directory or the repo — each test gets a fresh private directory and leaves nothing behind.
source: pytest docs: How to use temporary directories

claim: Modify environment variables, attributes, and paths through the monkeypatch fixture — every change is automatically undone after the test, unlike manual os.environ edits that leak into the next test.
Register custom markers in configuration and enable strict_markers — an unregistered marker is otherwise a silent no-op, so a typo like @pytest.mark.slowtest quietly stops deselecting anything.
source: pytest docs: How to mark test functions

key: General|Testing|Flakiness|Flaky tests are bugs not noise
claim: Treat a flaky test as a bug with a root cause — shared state, ordering dependence, real time, real network, or unawaited concurrency; blanket auto-rerun plugins hide exactly the failures that matter in production.

key: General|Testing|Skips|xfail for known bugs skip for environments
claim: Mark known-broken cases xfail (with the bug reference) so they flip to XPASS when fixed, and reserve skip/skipif for genuinely inapplicable environments — skipping a failing test erases the signal that it fails.
source: pytest docs: How to use skip and xfail
